planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in spring after last frost in well-drained soil with full sun; avoid disturbing the root system during planting as butia is sensitive to root damage.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained soil; the jelly palm is one of the hardiest feather palms in cultivation, tolerating temperatures to about 12°F (-11°C) with minimal damage, making it reliable across zone 8b-10. The silvery-blue arching fronds give a dramatic tropical appearance while the plant chugs through drought and poor soils with little supplemental care once established. Clusters of small orange-yellow fruits ripen in summer and have a sweet-tart apricot-pineapple flavor prized for jellies, wine, and juice — harvest when the entire cluster begins to drop. Water deeply but infrequently during the first two years to encourage deep root development, then reduce irrigation significantly.

planting.note: Plant container-grown palms in spring in a wind-sheltered full-sun location with fertile, well-drained soil; in zone 9 choose a warm microclimate near a south-facing wall to protect from brief freezes.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile soil with consistent moisture; the ribbon fan palm is prized for its enormous fan leaves whose outer leaflets droop into graceful, ribbon-like weeping tips that set it apart from other fan palms. Mature specimens can reach 50 feet with individual leaves spanning 10 feet, making this an architectural statement palm for large landscapes and parks. It is among the more cold-tolerant of the Livistona genus and can handle brief dips into the upper 20s°F when established in a sheltered microclimate. Fertilize three times per year with a slow-release palm fertilizer high in magnesium and manganese to maintain the deep green frond color.

planting.note: Plant in spring in extremely well-drained or rocky soil in full sun; avoid any soil that holds moisture as this palm of dry limestone mountains is prone to root rot in poorly drained sites.

directions: Plant in full sun in fast-draining, lean soil — this native of dry Mexican limestone mountains thrives on neglect and is one of the most drought-tolerant palms available for zone 8-9 gardens. The silvery-blue fan leaves are similar to other Brahea species and provide striking year-round color; the palm is slow-growing but extremely long-lived. Small, dark, date-like fruits are produced in pendant clusters and are sweet and edible, though they are used mainly by local communities in Mexico for food, wine, and traditional crafts. Once established, reduce irrigation to near zero; overwatering is the most common cause of failure with this species.

planting.note: Plant balled-and-burlapped or container-grown trees in fall while soils are still warm to allow root establishment before winter; if transplanting seed, sow fresh nuts immediately after collection in fall as they lose viability rapidly when dried.

directions: Plant in moist, deep, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; Ohio buckeye is the state tree of Ohio and a striking native specimen tree with large, palmately compound leaves that emerge very early in spring and put on a vivid orange-red fall color display — often the first tree to color and drop leaves in autumn. Upright panicles of yellow-green flowers appear in April and May, attracting early-emerging pollinators, and ripen into the distinctive spiny husks that split to reveal the rich brown nuts that give the tree its name. All parts of the plant — bark, leaves, and especially the nuts — are toxic to humans, livestock, and dogs and should not be planted where children or grazing animals have access. Provide consistent moisture, especially during establishment, as it is poorly adapted to drought and will drop leaves early under heat stress.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in sandy or well-drained soil in full to partial sun; scarify or nick seed coat lightly before sowing to improve germination, and inoculate with appropriate Bradyrhizobium rhizobial inoculant to activate nitrogen fixation.

directions: Direct sow in sandy or gravelly, low-fertility soil in full to partial sun after last frost; this twining native legume vine thrives in the poor, dry, sandy soils of the southeastern coastal plain where most garden plants fail, and fixes its own nitrogen through root nodule bacteria. Slender twining stems produce small pink-purple pea flowers from midsummer through early fall that are visited by native bees, and the vine is a documented larval host plant for gray hairstreak and other butterfly species, making it valuable in wildlife and pollinator gardens. It is a true perennial in zones 7-10, dying back to the roots after frost and re-emerging reliably from the root crown in spring. Avoid amending soil with compost or fertilizer as rich soils reduce flowering and encourage excessive foliage at the expense of blooms and seed production.
